rationale_for_baseline_tier: A 62-year-old with ~45 minutes of ongoing crushing central chest pain at rest, radiating to the left arm and jaw, with drenching diaphoresis, vomiting, severe breathlessness and a sense of impending doom — the cardinal presentation of an acute coronary syndrome / evolving myocardial infarction. The dominant early hazard is malignant ventricular arrhythmia and sudden cardiac death, and the telehealth clinician can neither examine the patient, obtain a 12-lead ECG or troponin, nor provide monitoring or a defibrillator. This is an unequivocal T5 — call 000 for an emergency ambulance to a PCI-capable centre — with chewable aspirin given if available while awaiting transport. There is no lower acceptable tier: self-transport or GP deferral risks cardiac arrest without resuscitation capability.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Ongoing crushing chest pain at rest with radiation to the arm/jaw and a sense of impending doom
escalation_edges.rationale: A prolonged ischaemic-type chest pain with high-risk features is a time-critical emergency; every minute of delay to reperfusion costs myocardium and raises the risk of fatal arrhythmia.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This could be a heart attack. I want you to call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them he has severe crushing chest pain spreading to his arm and jaw — do not wait to see if it eases.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening breathlessness, drenching sweat, further vomiting or the pain intensifying while awaiting the ambulance
escalation_edges.rationale: Progressive autonomic collapse or worsening dyspnoea signals a large evolving infarct and possible early cardiogenic compromise requiring immediate paramedic intervention.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his breathing gets worse, he is sweating more, vomiting again, or the pain is getting stronger, stay on the line with 000 and tell them he is deteriorating.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Loss of consciousness / collapse with absent or abnormal (gasping) breathing
escalation_edges.rationale: Cardiac arrest from a malignant ventricular arrhythmia is the leading early cause of death in acute MI; immediate bystander CPR under dispatcher guidance is life-saving until paramedics arrive with a defibrillator.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he collapses and stops breathing normally or is only gasping, lay him flat on the floor and start pushing hard and fast in the centre of his chest — the 000 call-taker will count it out with you and tell you exactly what to do.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse is present as a layperson (not a health professional) and is frightened; there is no home BP cuff, oximeter or GTN in the house
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Use the spouse as the hands for calling 000, giving chewable aspirin if it is in the house, keeping the patient still, unlocking the door and, if needed, performing dispatcher-guided CPR — but do not expect device measurements or drug administration beyond aspirin, and keep instructions simple and calm.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: At home, dependent on ambulance response time for definitive care
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise calling 000 immediately rather than any attempt to self-transport, and unlocking the door so paramedics can reach him without delay.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 now for an emergency ambulance and tell them a 62-year-old man has severe crushing chest pain spreading to his left arm and jaw, is sweating heavily, has vomited and is short of breath — a suspected heart attack — and needs a priority response to a hospital that can open the artery. If there is aspirin in the house and he is not allergic to it and not bleeding, give him one 300 mg tablet to chew, but do not delay the 000 call to find it. Keep him sitting quietly at rest, stay with him, unlock the front door, and be ready to start CPR if he stops responding.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is a life-threatening emergency and must not wait for a GP or clinic appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Relay to the paramedics and receiving team the timeline (~45 minutes of ongoing rest chest pain), the radiation to the left arm and jaw, the diaphoresis, vomiting and breathlessness, whether aspirin was given and at what time, and that there is no prior cardiac history or regular medication.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Anything short of an immediate 000 emergency ambulance — reassuring the pain as indigestion or anxiety, recommending an antacid or watchful waiting, arranging a GP or same-day clinic review, or advising self-transport to an emergency department.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Unmonitored cardiac arrest from a ventricular arrhythmia at home or in a car with no defibrillator, or an expanding infarct with loss of the reperfusion window leading to death or severe heart failure.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing the patient to an emergency department but by self-transport rather than by emergency ambulance with monitoring and defibrillation capability.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The definitive diagnosis (STEMI vs NSTEMI vs the catastrophic mimic of aortic dissection) cannot be settled via telehealth without a 12-lead ECG and troponin.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Do not attempt to settle the diagnosis remotely; recognise a high-risk acute cardiac emergency, give chewable aspirin if available, and call 000 immediately for transfer to a PCI-capable centre where the ECG and troponin are done.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Reassuring the pain as indigestion or anxiety, advising an antacid or 'wait and see', asking the patient to obtain an ECG, or advising self-transport.
